words.shortDefinition: Cross-Site Scripting; a vulnerability that allows an attacker to inject malicious scripts into a web page.
words.simpleDefinition: Cross-Site Scripting — an attack where malicious code is injected into a trusted website and runs in other users' browsers, stealing data or hijacking sessions.
words.longDefinition: Occurs when an application includes untrusted data in a new web page without proper validation or escaping. These scripts can then bypass access controls such as the same-origin policy.
words.techContext: We implemented strict output encoding to prevent XSS attacks in our user comment section.
words.exampleSentence: The hacker used an XSS vulnerability to steal user session cookies by injecting a script into the search results page.
words.etymology: Acronym for Cross-Site Scripting (X used to distinguish from CSS).
words.connector: Think of XSS like someone slipping a forged note into a restaurant's menu board — every customer who reads the board sees the attacker's message as if it came from the restaurant.

codeExample.code:
Hello, ${userName}
userName = ""

words.shortDefinition: Cross-Site Request Forgery; an attack that forces a user to execute unwanted actions on a web app.
words.simpleDefinition: Cross-Site Request Forgery — an attack that tricks a logged-in user's browser into sending an unwanted request to a site they're authenticated on, performing actions without their knowledge.
words.longDefinition: Also known as Sea-Surf. It tricks a victim into submitting a malicious request, using the victim's own credentials (like session cookies) to perform an action on a site where they are currently authenticated.
words.techContext: We added CSRF tokens to all our forms to ensure that requests only originate from our own domain.
words.exampleSentence: A CSRF attack could allow a malicious site to transfer money out of a user's bank account if they are logged in.
words.connector: Think of CSRF like someone leaving a pre-signed blank cheque on your desk and waiting until you walk past the bank — your signature makes the action look legitimate, but you never intended it.

codeExample.code:
<!-- Protection: CSRF Token -->
<input type="hidden" name="_csrf" value="a7b9c3...">

<!-- Modern browser protection -->
Set-Cookie: session=123; SameSite=Lax;

words.shortDefinition: A vulnerability that allows an attacker to interfere with the queries that an application makes to its database.
words.simpleDefinition: An attack where malicious SQL code is inserted into an input field and executed by the database, letting attackers read, alter, or delete data they shouldn't access.
words.longDefinition: Happens when user-supplied data is directly concatenated into a SQL string. Attackers can use this to bypass authentication, view sensitive data, or even delete the entire database.
words.techContext: Always use parameterized queries or an ORM to eliminate the risk of SQL injection.
words.exampleSentence: The attacker performed a SQL injection by entering "' OR '1'='1" into the password field to bypass the login check.
words.connector: Think of SQL injection like slipping extra instructions into an order form — the staff follow the form literally, so the attacker's hidden instructions get carried out alongside the real order.

words.shortDefinition: A strategy for limiting network traffic to prevent service abuse.
words.simpleDefinition: A control that caps how many requests a client can make in a given time window, protecting the server from abuse and automated attacks.
words.longDefinition: Restricts the number of requests a user or an IP address can make to an API or website within a specific timeframe. This protects against brute-force attacks, scraping, and DDoS.
words.techContext: We implemented rate limiting on the login endpoint to allow only 5 attempts per minute per IP address.
words.exampleSentence: The user received a 429 'Too Many Requests' error because they exceeded the API's rate limiting policy.
words.connector: Think of rate limiting like a turnstile with a per-minute limit — you can enter, but if you try to rush through too fast, it stops you until your window resets.

words.shortDefinition: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing; a mechanism that allows restricted resources on a web page to be requested from another domain.
words.simpleDefinition: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing — a browser policy that controls which external domains are allowed to make requests to your server, preventing hostile sites from reading your users' data.
words.longDefinition: A security feature implemented by browsers that prevents scripts on one site from making requests to another site unless specifically allowed via HTTP headers.
words.techContext: We had to update the CORS policy on our backend to allow requests from our new staging domain.
words.exampleSentence: The frontend couldn't fetch data from the API because of a CORS error—the server hadn't whitelisted the web app's origin.
words.connector: Think of CORS like a club's guest list — requests from unknown origins are turned away at the door unless the server has explicitly added them to the allowed list.

words.shortDefinition: Content Security Policy; an added layer of security that helps detect and mitigate certain types of attacks.
words.simpleDefinition: Content Security Policy — an HTTP header that tells browsers which sources of scripts, styles, and other resources are trusted, blocking anything not on the allow-list.
words.longDefinition: A security header that tells the browser which sources of content (scripts, styles, images) are trusted. It is the most effective defense against XSS and clickjacking.
words.techContext: Our CSP prevents any inline scripts from running, effectively blocking most common XSS vectors.
words.exampleSentence: The analytics script failed to load because its domain wasn't included in the site's Content Security Policy.
words.connector: Think of CSP like a strict import policy — only goods from approved suppliers are allowed through; anything from an unknown source is turned away at the border.

words.shortDefinition: A fundamental security mechanism that restricts how a document or script loaded from one origin can interact with a resource from another origin.
words.simpleDefinition: A browser security rule that stops scripts on one website from reading or modifying data from a different website, unless that other site explicitly grants permission.
words.longDefinition: Abbreviated as SOP. It ensures that a script on 'site-a.com' cannot read sensitive data from 'site-b.com' (like a user's inbox) without explicit permission. CORS is the standard way to safely relax this policy.
words.techContext: Without the Same-Origin Policy, any website you visit could freely read your session cookies for other open tabs.
words.exampleSentence: The browser's same-origin policy blocked the iframe from accessing the parent window's local storage.
words.connector: Think of the same-origin policy like postal confidentiality — the post office won't let your neighbour open your letters just because you both live on the same street.

words.shortDefinition: HTTP Strict Transport Security; forces browsers to communicate with a server only over HTTPS.
words.simpleDefinition: HTTP Strict Transport Security — a header that tells browsers to always connect to a site over HTTPS, never falling back to unencrypted HTTP even if a link says otherwise.
words.longDefinition: A security header that prevents 'man-in-the-middle' attacks like SSL stripping. Once a browser sees this header, it will automatically convert all future HTTP links to that site into HTTPS locally.
words.techContext: We added our domain to the HSTS preload list so that browsers never even attempt an insecure connection to our site.
words.exampleSentence: HSTS ensures that even if a user manually types 'http://' in their browser, they are immediately redirected to a secure 'https://' connection.
words.etymology: Acronym for HTTP Strict Transport Security.
words.connector: Think of HSTS like a 'use the front door only' sign — the browser remembers and never tries the back door (HTTP) again, even if someone sends it there.

words.shortDefinition: The process of cleaning data to ensure it's safe to use.
words.simpleDefinition: The process of cleaning user input by removing or escaping potentially dangerous characters before using it, preventing injection attacks.
words.longDefinition: Involves removing or escaping potentially dangerous characters from user input (like <, >, or SQL keywords) before storing it in a database or displaying it in a UI.
words.techContext: We use a library called DOMPurify for HTML sanitization to ensure that user-provided bio descriptions don't contain malicious scripts.
words.exampleSentence: Proper sanitization of the search query prevents attackers from breaking out of the intended logic.
words.connector: Think of sanitization like a bouncer checking IDs for altered details — they strip anything suspicious before anyone gets in.

words.shortDefinition: An attack that tricks a user into clicking something different from what they perceive.
words.simpleDefinition: An attack that overlays an invisible malicious frame on top of a legitimate page, tricking users into clicking elements they can't see and performing unintended actions.
words.longDefinition: Also known as a 'UI redress attack'. An attacker uses multiple transparent or opaque layers to trick a user into clicking on a button or link on another page when they were intending to click on the top-level page.
words.techContext: We use the 'X-Frame-Options' header to prevent our site from being embedded in an iframe on malicious domains.
words.exampleSentence: The clickjacking attack tricked the user into deleting their account by placing an invisible 'Delete' button over a seemingly harmless 'Play' button.
words.connector: Think of clickjacking like placing a transparent overlay on an ATM keypad — the victim presses the real buttons, but the overlay records every press for the attacker.

words.shortDefinition: Distributed Denial of Service; an attempt to make an online service unavailable by overwhelming it with traffic from multiple sources.
words.simpleDefinition: Distributed Denial of Service — an attack that floods a server with traffic from many sources simultaneously, overwhelming it so legitimate users can't get through.
words.longDefinition: Attackers use a 'botnet' of compromised computers to flood a server with more requests than it can handle, causing it to crash or become unresponsive to legitimate users.
words.techContext: We moved our site behind Cloudflare to get automated DDoS protection and traffic scrubbing.
words.exampleSentence: The site was down for three hours yesterday due to a massive DDoS attack targeting our DNS servers.
words.etymology: Acronym for Distributed Denial of Service.
words.connector: Think of DDoS like thousands of prank callers all ringing a customer service line at once — real customers can't get through because every line is busy.

words.shortDefinition: Web Application Firewall; filters, monitors, and blocks HTTP traffic to and from a web application.
words.simpleDefinition: Web Application Firewall — a security layer that inspects HTTP traffic and blocks requests that match known attack patterns before they reach your application.
words.longDefinition: Unlike a regular firewall that focuses on network ports, a WAF operates at the application layer to detect and block common web attacks like SQL injection and XSS before they reach the server.
words.techContext: Our WAF automatically blocked several requests that looked like attempted path traversal attacks.
words.exampleSentence: We configured our AWS WAF with the core OWASP ruleset to provide an extra layer of defense for our production environment.
words.connector: Think of a WAF like a customs officer who knows every trick in the smuggler's handbook — they scan each package against a list of known contraband and stop the suspicious ones.

words.shortDefinition: The practice of testing a computer system to find security vulnerabilities that an attacker could exploit.
words.simpleDefinition: A controlled security exercise where authorised testers attempt to break into a system using real attack techniques, finding vulnerabilities before malicious actors do.
words.longDefinition: Also known as 'ethical hacking'. It involves an authorized simulated attack on the system to evaluate its security. The results are used to patch weaknesses before a real attacker finds them.
words.techContext: Our annual penetration testing report identified several low-risk vulnerabilities in our internal API.
words.exampleSentence: The pentesting team successfully gained access to the database by exploiting a misconfigured staging server.
words.etymology: from Latin penetrare ('to put into') + testing.
words.connector: Think of penetration testing like hiring a locksmith to try to break into your own house — if they can get in, a burglar probably can too, and now you know which lock to fix.

words.shortDefinition: Man-in-the-Middle; an attack where the attacker secretly intercepts and relays messages between two parties.
words.simpleDefinition: Man-in-the-Middle — an attack where a third party secretly intercepts and potentially alters communications between two parties who believe they're talking directly to each other.
words.longDefinition: The attacker makes independent connections with the victims and relays messages between them, making them believe they are talking directly to each other over a private connection, when in fact the entire conversation is controlled by the attacker.
words.techContext: Public WiFi networks are common targets for MITM attacks, which is why using a VPN or HTTPS is essential.
words.exampleSentence: Using HTTPS prevents MITM attacks by ensuring that the connection is encrypted and the server's identity is verified.
words.connector: Think of MITM like a dishonest interpreter translating between two foreign diplomats — each side believes they're communicating directly, but the interpreter changes whatever they want.

words.shortDefinition: HTTP cookies with attributes set to protect against unauthorized access.
words.simpleDefinition: Cookies marked with flags that restrict their exposure — Secure sends them only over HTTPS, HttpOnly hides them from JavaScript, SameSite limits cross-origin sending.
words.longDefinition: Attributes include 'HttpOnly' (prevents JS access, stopping XSS cookie theft), 'Secure' (only sent over HTTPS), and 'SameSite' (prevents CSRF by controlling cross-origin sending).
words.techContext: We updated our session cookie settings to include HttpOnly and SameSite=Lax for better security.
words.exampleSentence: Even though the site had an XSS vulnerability, the attacker couldn't steal the session cookie because it had the HttpOnly flag set.
words.connector: Think of secure cookie flags like a vault combination: Secure means only use the vault's private entrance, HttpOnly means the contents can't be photographed, SameSite means don't carry it to other buildings.

words.shortDefinition: Insecure Direct Object Reference; a vulnerability where an app provides direct access to objects based on user-supplied input.
words.simpleDefinition: Insecure Direct Object Reference — a vulnerability where an attacker changes a parameter (like a user ID in a URL) to access data they're not authorised to see.
words.longDefinition: Occurs when an application uses an identifier for direct access to an internal object but does not have an authorization check to ensure the user is allowed to access that specific object.
words.techContext: A classic IDOR bug is changing the 'id' in a URL from 101 to 102 and being able to see another user's private profile.
words.exampleSentence: We fixed an IDOR vulnerability where any logged-in user could view any invoice just by changing the invoice ID in the API request.
words.etymology: Acronym for Insecure Direct Object Reference.
words.connector: Think of IDOR like a hotel where every room number is the key — if room 302 works on room 303 too, anyone can walk into any room by just trying the next number.

words.shortDefinition: Server-Side Request Forgery; an attack where the server is tricked into making requests to an unintended location.
words.simpleDefinition: Server-Side Request Forgery — an attack where an attacker tricks a server into making HTTP requests on their behalf, often reaching internal systems the attacker can't access directly.
words.longDefinition: The attacker can force the server to make a connection to internal-only services (like a database or metadata API) that are not otherwise accessible from the outside.
words.techContext: We had to carefully whitelist the allowed domains for our 'fetch-from-url' feature to prevent SSRF attacks.
words.exampleSentence: The attacker used SSRF to query the AWS metadata service at 169.254.169.254 and steal the server's IAM credentials.
words.connector: Think of SSRF like persuading a trusted insider to post a letter for you to a restricted address — the server's privileged position lets the request get through where yours would be blocked.

words.shortDefinition: The principle that a user or process should have only the minimum access levels necessary to perform its job.
words.simpleDefinition: A security principle where every user, process, or system is given only the minimum permissions needed to do its job — nothing more.
words.longDefinition: A core security concept. It reduces the damage an attacker can do if they compromise a single component of your system. For example, the web server should not have permission to delete rows from the database.
words.techContext: Following the principle of least privilege, we created a dedicated database user for our application that only has 'SELECT' and 'INSERT' permissions.
words.exampleSentence: By applying least privilege to our CI/CD tokens, we ensured that a leaked token couldn't be used to delete our production infrastructure.
words.connector: Think of least privilege like an office key system — the post-room staff have a key to the post room, not to the CEO's office; each person gets only the doors they need.

words.shortDefinition: A security framework that requires all users to be authenticated and authorized before being granted access.
words.simpleDefinition: A security model that never automatically trusts any user or device, even inside the corporate network — every access request is verified every time.
words.longDefinition: Assumes that there is no implicit trust in the network; no user or device is trusted by default, even if they are inside the corporate 'perimeter'. Every request must be verified as if it originated from an open network.
words.techContext: Our company moved to a Zero Trust architecture, requiring MFA (Multi-Factor Authentication) for every internal tool access.
words.exampleSentence: In a Zero Trust environment, being on the office WiFi doesn't give you automatic access to the source code repository.
words.connector: Think of zero trust like a high-security building where you swipe your badge at every door, every time, even if you've just come from inside — being in the building doesn't grant free access.

words.shortDefinition: An attack that involves systematically checking all possible passwords until the correct one is found.
words.simpleDefinition: An attack method that systematically tries every possible password or key combination until the correct one is found, relying on computation speed rather than cleverness.
words.longDefinition: A simple but time-consuming method. It can be performed manually or automated using scripts. Modern systems protect against this using account lockouts, rate limiting, and CAPTCHAs.
words.techContext: The hacker attempted a brute force attack on our admin portal, but was blocked after 10 failed attempts.
words.exampleSentence: To defend against brute force, we require users to create complex passwords that would take thousands of years to crack.
words.connector: Think of brute force like trying every key on a giant keyring one by one — eventually one works, and the only defence is making the keyring impossibly large.

words.shortDefinition: The process of verifying that the data provided to an application is well-formed and valid.
words.simpleDefinition: Checking that data submitted by a user or external system matches the expected format, type, and range before processing it — rejecting anything that doesn't conform.
words.longDefinition: The most important defense against many web vulnerabilities. It involves checking that a user's age is a number, an email address has an @ symbol, and a string doesn't contain unexpected special characters.
words.techContext: We use a validation schema (like Zod or Joi) to ensure that all incoming API data matches our expected format.
words.exampleSentence: Strong input validation on the server side prevents users from submitting malicious payloads to our database.
words.connector: Think of input validation like a form with required fields and format checks — you can't submit a phone number field with letters, and you can't leave a required field blank.

words.shortDefinition: A security mechanism set as a trap for attackers.
words.simpleDefinition: A decoy system or field deliberately made to look attractive to attackers — any interaction with it is a clear sign of malicious intent, triggering alerts or bans.
words.longDefinition: A decoy system or data designed to look like a legitimate part of the infrastructure but is actually isolated and monitored. It is used to detect, deflect, or study hacking attempts.
words.techContext: We added a 'hidden' field to our registration form as a honeypot; any request that fills it out is immediately flagged as a bot.
words.exampleSentence: The attackers spent three days trying to crack our honeypot server while we observed their techniques from a safe distance.
words.etymology: Metaphor for a pot of honey that attracts flies (attackers).
words.connector: Think of a honeypot like a fake wallet left on a park bench with a tracker inside — the moment someone picks it up, you know exactly who the thief is.

words.shortDefinition: An attack that aims to access files and directories that are stored outside the web root folder.
words.simpleDefinition: An attack where an attacker manipulates file path inputs — using sequences like '../..' — to access files outside the intended directory, like reading server configuration or credentials.
words.longDefinition: By manipulating variables that reference files with 'dot-dot-slash' (../) sequences, an attacker may be able to access arbitrary files and directories stored on file system including application source code or sensitive system configuration files.
words.techContext: I added a check to ensure that the user-provided filename didn't contain any backslashes or dots to prevent path traversal.
words.exampleSentence: The attacker used a path traversal exploit to download the /etc/passwd file from our web server.
words.connector: Think of path traversal like sneaking through a museum by following maintenance corridors — the public doors only show you the galleries, but the back passages let you reach the archive.

words.shortDefinition: The process of converting a given key into another value.
words.simpleDefinition: Running data through a one-way function that produces a fixed-size fingerprint — used to verify integrity or store passwords without keeping the original value.
words.longDefinition: In security, a hash function takes an input (like a password) and produces a fixed-size string of characters. It is a one-way process; you cannot reverse a hash to get the original input. Common algorithms include SHA-256 and Argon2.
words.techContext: We never store raw passwords; we only store the hash of the password using the Argon2id algorithm.
words.exampleSentence: Even if our database is leaked, the users' passwords are safe because they are all protected by cryptographic hashing.
words.etymology: from Old French hacher ('to chop into small pieces').
words.connector: Think of hashing like a fingerprint — it uniquely identifies the person, but you can't reverse a fingerprint to reconstruct their face.

words.shortDefinition: A weakness in an information system that could be exploited by a threat source.
words.simpleDefinition: A weakness in a system — a coding error, misconfiguration, or design flaw — that an attacker could exploit to cause harm.
words.longDefinition: Can be a bug in the code, a mistake in the configuration, or a weakness in the system's architecture. Common vulnerabilities are tracked by the CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) system.
words.techContext: Our security scanner identified a high-severity vulnerability in an old version of a library we were using.
words.exampleSentence: We immediately released a patch after discovering a zero-day vulnerability in our payment processing module.
words.connector: Think of a vulnerability like a cracked window in a house — it's not a break-in yet, but it's an opening an intruder could use if they notice it before you fix it.
